meta.certificationName: Microsoft Certified: Azure MLOps Engineer Associate (AI-300)
meta.title: MS-AI300-3: Pipeline Reuse, Deployment Strategies, and Monitoring

questions.stem: Which Azure Machine Learning compute type is best suited to an individual engineer interactively developing notebooks?

options.text: A compute instance
options.explanation: Compute instances are intended for interactive development such as notebooks and ad hoc authoring.

options.text: A managed online endpoint
options.explanation: Online endpoints serve inference traffic, not notebook authoring.

options.text: A batch endpoint
options.explanation: Batch endpoints run asynchronous scoring jobs.

options.text: A model registry
options.explanation: A registry stores shareable assets rather than interactive compute.

questions.stem: A training workload must scale out for repeated jobs and stop when idle to control cost. Which compute target is the best default choice?

options.text: A compute cluster
options.explanation: Compute clusters are the standard elastic target for scheduled and repeated training jobs.

options.text: A compute instance
options.explanation: Instances are better for interactive work than pooled training jobs.

options.text: A local laptop attached as a target
options.explanation: A local machine is not the best managed default for scalable production jobs.

options.text: A model registry
options.explanation: Registries are not execution targets.

questions.stem: Why are Azure ML pipeline components valuable in MLOps?

options.text: They make steps reusable and versionable across pipelines
options.explanation: Components package inputs, outputs, commands, and environments so steps can be reused and versioned.

options.text: They remove the need for environments
options.explanation: Components still reference environments.

options.text: They are only for low-code Designer use
options.explanation: They are usable from YAML and SDK, not only Designer.

options.text: They automatically deploy every model to production
options.explanation: Deployment remains a separate stage.

questions.stem: A team wants the same pipeline definition to run against different input paths and hyperparameters without editing code. Which design supports that best?

options.text: Declare pipeline inputs and runtime parameters
options.explanation: Pipeline parameters let the same definition run flexibly across environments and inputs.

options.text: Hard-code every path and setting in the script
options.explanation: Hard-coding hurts reuse.

options.text: Create a new workspace for each dataset
options.explanation: Separate workspaces are unnecessary for parameter variation.

options.text: Use only manual Studio clicks
options.explanation: Manual clicks are not the most reproducible design.

questions.stem: Which data asset type is intended for tabular or structured data with schema semantics in Azure ML?

options.text: MLTable
options.explanation: MLTable is designed for structured datasets with transformations and schema information.

options.text: URI file
options.explanation: URI file is a generic file reference rather than a tabular abstraction.

options.text: Docker environment
options.explanation: An environment packages software dependencies.

options.text: Managed online endpoint
options.explanation: An online endpoint is for inference serving.

questions.stem: An engineer wants a framework-agnostic Python prediction interface after loading a registered model. Which MLflow flavor should they prefer?

options.text: `mlflow.pyfunc.load_model`
options.explanation: The pyfunc flavor provides a generic prediction interface independent of the original training library.

options.text: `mlflow.tensorflow.load_model` for every model
options.explanation: The TensorFlow flavor is framework specific.

options.text: `mlflow.sklearn.load_model` for every model
options.explanation: The sklearn flavor is framework specific.

options.text: A raw blob download with no MLflow flavor
options.explanation: Skipping MLflow flavors loses the generic packaged interface.

questions.stem: What is the key advantage of a managed online endpoint in Azure ML?

options.text: It supports real-time inference with deployment management handled by Azure ML
options.explanation: Managed online endpoints are Azure-managed real-time serving endpoints.

options.text: It is only for offline scoring of very large datasets
options.explanation: Offline scoring is the role of batch endpoints.

options.text: It cannot route traffic between deployments
options.explanation: Traffic splitting is supported across deployments.

options.text: It requires Kubernetes administration by default
options.explanation: Managed endpoints reduce the need for direct cluster administration.

questions.stem: A team wants blue-green rollout for a new model version with 10% traffic before full cutover. Which capability supports that?

options.text: Multiple deployments behind one online endpoint with traffic splitting
options.explanation: Online endpoints support multiple deployments and configurable traffic percentages for phased rollout.

options.text: A batch endpoint job schedule
options.explanation: Batch endpoints are asynchronous and not designed for live traffic split.

options.text: A Responsible AI dashboard widget
options.explanation: The RAI dashboard is for assessment, not request routing.

options.text: A datastore secret rotation
options.explanation: Secret rotation is unrelated to rollout strategy.

questions.stem: When is a batch endpoint usually a better fit than an online endpoint?

options.text: When scoring large datasets asynchronously without a low-latency interactive requirement
options.explanation: Batch endpoints are optimized for asynchronous large-scale scoring.

options.text: When every request must return in milliseconds
options.explanation: Millisecond interactive requests point to online endpoints.

options.text: When the team needs traffic splitting for web inference
options.explanation: Traffic splitting is an online endpoint pattern.

options.text: When no input data exists yet
options.explanation: Endpoints do not solve the absence of input data.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of an Azure ML registry in MLOps?

options.text: To share and govern reusable ML assets such as models and components across workspaces
options.explanation: Registries centralize reusable assets for promotion and sharing across workspaces.

options.text: To serve public HTTPS traffic directly
options.explanation: Serving is handled by endpoints.

options.text: To replace Git repositories
options.explanation: Git still manages source control.

options.text: To run notebook kernels
options.explanation: Notebook execution is handled by compute resources.

questions.stem: A model passes evaluation in dev and should move to broader reuse with version history and tags. Which action aligns best with that goal?

options.text: Register or promote the model in a registry with metadata
options.explanation: Registries support versioning, metadata, and controlled promotion of ML assets.

options.text: Create a new local folder on one engineer laptop
options.explanation: A local folder is not a governed promotion mechanism.

options.text: Delete the training run
options.explanation: Deleting the run removes lineage.

options.text: Convert the model to an MLTable
options.explanation: MLTable is for data assets, not model promotion.

questions.stem: What does Azure ML model monitoring help detect?

options.text: Data quality or prediction drift over time
options.explanation: Model monitoring focuses on data quality, drift, and related production behavior changes.

options.text: Whether a user likes the UI theme
options.explanation: UI preference is unrelated.

options.text: Whether Git branches were named well
options.explanation: Branch naming is a source-control concern.

options.text: Whether Docker is installed on a laptop
options.explanation: Laptop Docker installation is not the purpose of model monitoring.

questions.stem: A production team wants a baseline from training data and scheduled checks with alerts when serving inputs drift materially. Which Azure ML feature set addresses this?

options.text: Model monitoring jobs with configured baselines and alerting integrations
options.explanation: Azure ML monitoring supports scheduled jobs, baselines, and alerting for production drift and quality signals.

options.text: Only notebook comments stored beside the run
options.explanation: Notebook comments are not monitoring automation.

options.text: A single manual dashboard screenshot
options.explanation: A screenshot is not an operational control.

options.text: An online endpoint traffic split
options.explanation: Traffic split helps rollout but not drift detection itself.

questions.stem: What is the Responsible AI dashboard primarily used for?

options.text: Inspecting fairness, explanations, error patterns, and related model behaviors
options.explanation: The Responsible AI dashboard brings together explanations, fairness, error analysis, and similar assessments.

options.text: Creating Kubernetes clusters
options.explanation: Cluster creation is unrelated.

options.text: Training foundation models from scratch
options.explanation: It does not train foundation models.

options.text: Replacing MLflow tracking
options.explanation: It complements rather than replaces experiment tracking.

questions.stem: A generative workflow built in Azure wants prompt orchestration, testing, and execution of flow graphs. Which tool family is most aligned?

options.text: Prompt flow
options.explanation: Prompt flow is designed for authoring and evaluating prompt-based AI flows.

options.text: Batch endpoints
options.explanation: Batch endpoints are for asynchronous scoring jobs.

options.text: Model registry tags
options.explanation: Registry tags are metadata only.

options.text: Datastore credentials
options.explanation: Datastore credentials are storage configuration.

questions.stem: Which TWO choices improve reproducibility of Azure ML training jobs? (Select TWO)

options.text: Pin the environment and package dependencies
options.explanation: Pinned environments help ensure the same software stack across runs.

options.text: Keep component and pipeline definitions versioned
options.explanation: Versioned components and pipelines improve traceability and reuse.

options.text: Manually edit notebooks in production without source control
options.explanation: Untracked notebook edits reduce reproducibility.

options.text: Change runtime parameters silently between runs without tracking
options.explanation: Untracked parameter changes undermine comparability.

questions.stem: A bank must host inference inside its own AKS environment because of enterprise network controls. Which endpoint style is the best fit?

options.text: A Kubernetes online endpoint on AKS
options.explanation: Kubernetes online endpoints support AKS-based serving for organizations that need cluster-level control.

options.text: A managed online endpoint with no network planning
options.explanation: Managed endpoints reduce ops but may not satisfy that AKS requirement.

options.text: A batch endpoint only
options.explanation: Batch endpoints are asynchronous rather than interactive.

options.text: A registry-only deployment
options.explanation: A registry stores assets but does not serve them.

questions.stem: Why do Azure ML environments matter in CI/CD?

options.text: They package consistent runtime dependencies for repeatable jobs and deployments
options.explanation: Environments define the runtime stack used by jobs and deployments, which is central to repeatability.

options.text: They replace every need for Docker or conda metadata
options.explanation: They often rely on Docker and dependency specs rather than replacing them conceptually.

options.text: They are only visual labels in Studio
options.explanation: They have operational effect beyond labeling.

options.text: They can be ignored once a model is registered
options.explanation: Registered models still need a serving environment.

questions.stem: What is a practical role for GitHub Actions in Azure ML MLOps?

options.text: Automating training, validation, or deployment workflows from repository events
options.explanation: GitHub Actions can orchestrate CI/CD pipelines that trigger Azure ML jobs and deployments.

options.text: Replacing the Azure ML control plane
options.explanation: Azure ML remains the ML platform control plane.

options.text: Serving real-time predictions directly
options.explanation: Predictions are served by endpoints.

options.text: Becoming the model registry itself
options.explanation: Actions do not replace a registry.

questions.stem: An engineer needs a folder of files as pipeline input with no table semantics. Which data asset abstraction is usually sufficient?

options.text: URI folder
options.explanation: A URI folder is the standard abstraction for folder-based file inputs without table behavior.

options.text: MLTable
options.explanation: MLTable is for structured/tabular semantics.

options.text: Managed online endpoint
options.explanation: An endpoint is a serving resource, not a dataset abstraction.

options.text: Traffic rule
options.explanation: Traffic rules apply to deployments, not data assets.

questions.stem: A pipeline must fan out scoring across partitions and then merge outputs. Which design is most aligned with Azure ML capabilities?

options.text: Use reusable components and parallel-capable pipeline steps
options.explanation: Azure ML pipelines support reusable components and patterns for parallelizable job design.

options.text: Put every partition in a single monolithic script step by hand
options.explanation: A monolithic hand-built step is harder to scale and govern.

options.text: Avoid versioning so each run can change freely
options.explanation: Avoiding versioning hurts MLOps discipline.

options.text: Use only a model registry with no pipeline job
options.explanation: A registry alone does not execute pipelines.

options.text: A model registry
